## Discharging the Post-Hoc Rule Selection

Model B's candidate rules are picked by inspecting the stimulus layout, which makes them empirical rather than prior. We discharge this in two complementary ways.

### Option 1: Sensitivity to the Candidate Rule Set

We define three alternative candidate rule sets and refit Model B on the same data:

* **Set A** — the 7 hand-picked rules (the ones in the Stan code above: 5 flat + 2 nested).
* **Set B** — 5 rules generated *blind* by running Model A on a separately-simulated training dataset and taking its top-weighted rules at the end of training. The training data is generated with $\varepsilon = 0.1$ on a different schedule than the fitting data. This is the "honest" version of the candidate set.
* **Set C** — 5 rules deliberately chosen to be *bad* (rules that disagree with most stimuli), as a stress test.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Helper to build a Stan model from a list of (predictor function) rules
build_stan_with_rules <- function(rule_predictors, name_suffix) {
  R <- length(rule_predictors)
  rule_lines <- paste(
    sprintf("    int pred%d = %s;", seq_len(R), rule_predictors),
    collapse = "\n"
  )
  lp_lines_model <- paste(
    sprintf("    lp[%d] = bernoulli_lpmf(y[i] | pred%d == 1 ? 1 - error_prob : error_prob);",
            seq_len(R), seq_len(R)),
    collapse = "\n"
  )

  stan_code <- sprintf("
data {
  int<lower=1> ntrials;
  int<lower=1> nfeatures;
  array[ntrials] int<lower=0, upper=1> y;
  array[ntrials, nfeatures] real obs;
  real prior_logit_error_mean;
  real<lower=0> prior_logit_error_sd;
}
parameters { real logit_error_prob; }
transformed parameters {
  real<lower=0, upper=1> error_prob = inv_logit(logit_error_prob);
  array[ntrials] real lp_marginal;
  for (i in 1:ntrials) {
    real h = obs[i, 1];
    real p = obs[i, 2];
%s
    int R = %d;
    vector[R] lp;
%s
    lp_marginal[i] = log_sum_exp(lp) - log(R);
  }
}
model {
  target += normal_lpdf(logit_error_prob | prior_logit_error_mean, prior_logit_error_sd);
  for (i in 1:ntrials) target += lp_marginal[i];
}
generated quantities {
  array[ntrials] real log_lik;
  for (i in 1:ntrials) log_lik[i] = lp_marginal[i];
  real lprior = normal_lpdf(logit_error_prob | prior_logit_error_mean, prior_logit_error_sd);
}
", rule_lines, R, lp_lines_model)

  fname <- here("stan", sprintf("ch14_rule_%s.stan",  name_suffix))
  write_stan_file(stan_code, dir = "stan/",
                  basename = sprintf("ch14_rule_%s.stan", name_suffix))
  cmdstan_model(fname, force_recompile = TRUE)
}

# Set A: hand-picked rules — 5 flat + 2 nested (replicates Model B exactly)
rules_A <- c(
  "h > 2.5 ? 1 : 0",
  "p < 2.5 ? 1 : 0",
  "(h > 2.0 && p > 3.0) ? 1 : 0",
  "(h < 2.0 || p < 2.0) ? 0 : 1",
  "(h > 2.5 && p < 3.5) ? 1 : 0",
  "(h > 3.5 || (h > 1.5 && p < 1.5)) ? 1 : 0",
  "(h > 1.5 && (p < 2.5 || h > 3.5)) ? 1 : 0"
)

# Set B: blind rules from a fresh Model A run on a separate seed
get_blind_rules <- function(seed = 7) {
  blind_sched <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = 50000 + seed)
  obs_b   <- as.matrix(blind_sched[, c("height", "position")])
  cat_b   <- blind_sched$category_feedback
  res     <- track_rule_learning(
    n_particles = N_PARTICLES_FIXED, error_prob = 0.1,
    obs = obs_b, cat_one = cat_b, max_dims = MAX_RULE_DIMS, top_k = 5
  )
  # Take the top 5 distinct rules at the final trial
  final <- res |> filter(trial == max(trial)) |>
    distinct(dimension1, operation1, threshold1,
             dimension2, operation2, threshold2,
             logic, prediction, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
    head(5)

  pred_strings <- character(nrow(final))
  for (k in seq_len(nrow(final))) {
    r <- final[k, ]
    feat1 <- if (r$dimension1 == 1) "h" else "p"
    cond1 <- sprintf("%s %s %.2f", feat1, r$operation1, r$threshold1)
    if (is.na(r$dimension2)) {
      pred_strings[k] <- sprintf("(%s) ? %d : %d", cond1, r$prediction, 1 - r$prediction)
    } else {
      feat2 <- if (r$dimension2 == 1) "h" else "p"
      cond2 <- sprintf("%s %s %.2f", feat2, r$operation2, r$threshold2)
      logic_op <- if (r$logic == "AND") "&&" else "||"
      pred_strings[k] <- sprintf("(%s %s %s) ? %d : %d",
                                  cond1, logic_op, cond2,
                                  r$prediction, 1 - r$prediction)
    }
  }
  pred_strings
}

rules_B <- get_blind_rules(seed = 7)

# Set C: deliberately bad rules
rules_C <- c(
  "h > 4.5 ? 1 : 0",        # virtually never fires
  "p > 4.5 ? 1 : 0",        # virtually never fires
  "h < 0.5 ? 1 : 0",        # virtually never fires
  "(h > 0.5 && p > 0.5) ? 1 : 0",  # always fires
  "(h > 0.5 || p > 0.5) ? 1 : 0"   # always fires
)

# Compile and fit each
mod_A <- build_stan_with_rules(rules_A, "setA")
mod_B <- build_stan_with_rules(rules_B, "setB")
mod_C <- build_stan_with_rules(rules_C, "setC")

fit_with_rules <- function(model, label) {
  fit <- model$sample(
    data = rule_data_single, init = 0.5, seed = 7,
    chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
    iter_warmup = 800, iter_sampling = 1000, refresh = 0, adapt_delta = 0.9
  )
  draws <- as_draws_df(fit$draws("error_prob"))
  tibble(
    rule_set     = label,
    posterior_mean = mean(draws$error_prob),
    posterior_lo = quantile(draws$error_prob, 0.025),
    posterior_hi = quantile(draws$error_prob, 0.975),
    elpd_loo     = loo::loo(fit$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))$estimates["elpd_loo", "Estimate"]
  )
}

rule_set_sensitivity_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_rule_set_sensitivity.rds")
if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(rule_set_sensitivity_file)) {
  rule_set_results <- bind_rows(
    fit_with_rules(mod_A, "A: hand-picked"),
    fit_with_rules(mod_B, "B: blind from Model A"),
    fit_with_rules(mod_C, "C: deliberately bad")

  saveRDS(rule_set_results, rule_set_sensitivity_file)
} else {

}
```

**Reading the rule-set sensitivity.** If the three posteriors agree, the candidate-set choice is innocuous. If they disagree — which is the expected outcome — the gap quantifies how much of Model B's estimated $\varepsilon$ is *cognitive* (genuinely tracking the agent's error rate) vs *structural* (compensating for missing rules). Set C should produce a wildly inflated $\varepsilon$ and a much worse ELPD, providing the upper bound on how much rule-set choice can move the posterior.

### Sensitivity to `max_dims` (Rule Grammar Depth)

### Sensitivity to Resampling Threshold

The chapter fixes `RESAMPLE_THRESHOLD = 0.5`. This controls how aggressively the particle filter reorganises its hypothesis set: lower values allow more particle degeneracy before resampling; higher values trigger resampling earlier. The cognitive interpretation is something like "how quickly the learner abandons low-performing hypotheses."


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
resample_sensitivity_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_resample_threshold_sensitivity.rds")
if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(resample_sensitivity_file)) {
  cat("Sweeping resampling threshold...\n")
  resample_results <- map_dfr(c(0.25, 0.50, 0.75), function(rt) {
    accs <- vapply(1:10, function(rep) {
      sched <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = 42000 + rep)
      sim <- rule_particle_filter(
        n_particles               = N_PARTICLES_FIXED,
        error_prob                = 0.1,
        obs                       = as.matrix(sched[, c("height", "position")]),
        cat_one                   = sched$category_feedback,
        max_dims                  = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
        resample_threshold_factor = rt,
        quiet                     = TRUE
      )
      mean(sim$sim_response == sched$category_feedback)
    }, numeric(1))
    tibble(resample_threshold = rt,
           mean_accuracy = mean(accs), sd_accuracy = sd(accs))
  })
  saveRDS(resample_results, resample_sensitivity_file)
} else {
  resample_results <- readRDS(resample_sensitivity_file)
}

if (exists("resample_results")) {
  print(resample_results)
  cat("If mean accuracy varies substantially across thresholds,\n",
      "the resampling threshold is a consequential structural choice.\n",
      "If it is roughly stable, the choice is innocuous for this task.\n")
}
```

### Option 2: Treat Set A as a Sensitivity Condition, Not a Canonical Model

Rather than use Set A as *the* Model B and add Set B as a "robustness check," the honest framing is to report all three sets as variants of Model B with no privileged status. The reader sees three posteriors instead of one and decides for themselves how much of the variation is structural. This is the framing we adopt for the chapter 15 model comparison, where Model B is reported as a *family* of proxies indexed by the candidate set rather than as a single point estimate.

## Calibration: Two Different Checks for Two Different Things

Earlier drafts of this chapter ran one block under the header "Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC)" that generated data with the particle filter (Model A) and fitted Model B. **That is not SBC.** Talts–Vehtari SBC requires the data to be drawn from the *same* generative model that is then fitted, so that the joint $(\theta, y)$ distribution of the inference model is what gets calibrated. When the generative and inferential models differ, what you are running is closer to a robustness check than a calibration check, and labelling it SBC is misleading.

We split the misnamed block into two correctly-labelled checks: (1) a **Cross-Model Calibration Check** (data from Model A, fitted by Model B) and (2) a **Real SBC for Model B** (data from Model B itself, fitted by Model B). The first is a robustness check; the second is the actual Talts–Vehtari calibration test.

### Cross-Model Calibration Check (NOT SBC)

**Procedure.** For each iteration $i = 1, \ldots, S$: draw $\varepsilon_i$ from the prior, simulate one dataset with Model A (the particle filter) using $\varepsilon_i$, fit Model B to that dataset, record the rank of $\varepsilon_i$ within Model B's posterior. Compute the rank ECDF.

**What a flat ECDF means here.** It means: under the specific misspecification of Model B for data from Model A, $\varepsilon$ happens to be recovered with the right *marginal* coverage. It does **not** mean Model B is calibrated in the Talts–Vehtari sense, because the joint $(\theta, y)$ distribution of the inference model is not the joint distribution of the data-generating process.

**Why we run it anyway.** Because it answers a useful practical question: "when I fit Model B to data that came from a richer generative process (Model A), are the resulting credible intervals approximately well-calibrated for the *cognitive* parameter $\varepsilon$?" The answer to that question is informative even though it is not what SBC is technically supposed to answer.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (run_intensive_checks) {
  n_cross_iters <- 500   # use >= 1000 for production

  cross_generator <- SBC_generator_function(
    function() {
      logit_eps_true <- rnorm(1, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
      eps_true       <- plogis(logit_eps_true)

      sched <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = sample.int(1e8, 1))
      # NOTE: data drawn from Model A (the particle filter)
      sim <- rule_particle_filter(
        n_particles = N_PARTICLES_FIXED,
        error_prob  = eps_true,
        obs         = as.matrix(sched[, c("height", "position")]),
        cat_one     = sched$category_feedback,
        max_dims    = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
        quiet       = TRUE
      )

      list(
        variables = list(logit_error_prob = logit_eps_true),
        generated = list(
          ntrials                = nrow(sched),
          nfeatures              = 2L,
          y                      = as.integer(sim$sim_response),
          obs                    = as.matrix(sched[, c("height", "position")]),
          prior_logit_error_mean = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN,
          prior_logit_error_sd   = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD
        )
      )
    }
  )

  cross_backend <- SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_rule_stan,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
  )

  cross_datasets <- generate_datasets(
    cross_generator,
    n_sims = n_cross_iters
  )

  cross_results <- compute_SBC(
    cross_datasets, cross_backend,
    cache_mode     = "results",
    cache_location = here("simdata", "ch14_cross_model_check_cache"),
    keep_fits      = FALSE
  )

  # Plotted as rank ECDF, but with explicit re-labelling in the title
  p1 <- plot_ecdf_diff(cross_results) +
    ggtitle("CROSS-MODEL CALIBRATION CHECK (not SBC)",
            subtitle = "Data: Model A. Fit: Model B. Flat ≠ calibrated, only marginally well-covered.")
  p2 <- plot_rank_hist(cross_results) +
    ggtitle("Cross-model rank histogram (not SBC)")
  print(p1 / p2)
}
```
:::


**Reading this plot.** A flat curve here means Model B's $\varepsilon$ posterior covers Model A's true generating $\varepsilon$ at approximately the right marginal rate. This is *useful* — it tells you Model B is at least not catastrophically wrong as a tool for estimating cognitive error rates. But it does not say Model B's inference engine is calibrated in the Talts–Vehtari sense, because the data-generating distribution is not the inference model's joint distribution.

### Real SBC for Model B

For a proper Talts–Vehtari SBC of Model B, the generator must use Model B itself: draw $\varepsilon$ from the prior, draw a rule index from $\{1, \ldots, 5\}$ uniformly (mirroring Model B's mixture prior), and generate Bernoulli responses accordingly. Then fit Model B and check the rank distribution.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (run_intensive_checks) {
  n_real_sbc_iters <- 500   # use >= 1000 for production

  real_sbc_generator <- SBC_generator_function(
    function() {
      logit_eps_true <- rnorm(1, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
      eps_true       <- plogis(logit_eps_true)

      # Use the SAME generator as Model B's mixture: draw a rule, generate Bernoulli responses
      rule_idx <- sample.int(7L, 1)
      sched    <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = sample.int(1e8, 1))
      obs_mat  <- as.matrix(sched[, c("height", "position")])
      n_trials <- nrow(obs_mat)

      rule_pred_for_trial <- function(i) {
        h <- obs_mat[i, 1]; p <- obs_mat[i, 2]
        switch(rule_idx,
               as.integer(h > 2.5),
               as.integer(p < 2.5),
               as.integer(h > 2.0 && p > 3.0),
               as.integer(!(h < 2.0 || p < 2.0)),
               as.integer(h > 2.5 && p < 3.5),
               as.integer(h > 3.5 || (h > 1.5 && p < 1.5)),
               as.integer(h > 1.5 && (p < 2.5 || h > 3.5)))
      }
      preds  <- vapply(seq_len(n_trials), rule_pred_for_trial, integer(1))
      p_cat1 <- ifelse(preds == 1, 1 - eps_true, eps_true)
      y      <- rbinom(n_trials, 1, p_cat1)

      list(
        variables = list(logit_error_prob = logit_eps_true),
        generated = list(
          ntrials                = n_trials,
          nfeatures              = 2L,
          y                      = as.integer(y),
          obs                    = obs_mat,
          prior_logit_error_mean = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN,
          prior_logit_error_sd   = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD
        )
      )
    }
  )

  real_sbc_backend <- SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_rule_stan,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
  )

  real_sbc_datasets <- generate_datasets(
    real_sbc_generator,
    n_sims = n_real_sbc_iters
  )

  real_sbc_results <- compute_SBC(
    real_sbc_datasets, real_sbc_backend,
    cache_mode     = "results",
    cache_location = here("simdata", "ch14_real_sbc_cache"),
    keep_fits      = FALSE
  )

  p1 <- plot_ecdf_diff(real_sbc_results) +
    ggtitle("REAL SBC for Model B (Talts–Vehtari)",
            subtitle = "Data: Model B's own mixture generator. Fit: Model B.")
  p2 <- plot_rank_hist(real_sbc_results) +
    ggtitle("Real SBC rank histogram for Model B")
  print(p1 / p2)
}
```
:::


**Reading this plot.** This is the actual calibration check. A flat ECDF here means Stan correctly inverts Model B (Model B's inference engine is calibrated in the Talts–Vehtari sense). Systematic deviations would indicate a miscalibration in the Stan implementation itself — a sign error, an off-by-one in the marginalisation, or a prior-on-`logit_error_prob` mismatch. **This is the only diagnostic in the chapter that tests the Stan engine on its own terms.** The cross-model check above tests something else — it tests how Model B behaves when applied to data from a richer process — and that something else is informative but it is not SBC.


### Posterior SBC for Model B (Phase 6c)

We need to perform **Posterior SBC (PSBC)** (see @sec-model-quality-checks): instead of drawing true parameters from the prior, draw them from the *fitted posterior*. This tests calibration in the specific high-probability region the actual data force us into. A sampler can be globally unbiased (prior SBC passes) yet struggle locally (PSBC fails) or vice versa.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (run_intensive_checks) {
  n_psbc_iters <- 500  # use >= 1000 for production

  if (!is.null(fit_rule_stan)) {
    # Draw true parameters from the fitted posterior, not the prior
    fitted_draws <- as_draws_df(fit_rule_stan$draws("logit_error_prob"))
    fitted_logit_eps <- fitted_draws$logit_error_prob

    psbc_generator <- SBC_generator_function(
      function() {
        # Draw from the POSTERIOR, not the prior
        logit_eps_true <- sample(fitted_logit_eps, 1)
        eps_true       <- plogis(logit_eps_true)

        # Generate from Model B's own mixture (same as real SBC; 7 rules)
        rule_idx <- sample.int(7L, 1)
        sched    <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = sample.int(1e8, 1))
        obs_mat  <- as.matrix(sched[, c("height", "position")])
        n_trials <- nrow(obs_mat)

        rule_pred_for_trial <- function(j) {
          h <- obs_mat[j, 1]; p <- obs_mat[j, 2]
          switch(rule_idx,
                 as.integer(h > 2.5),
                 as.integer(p < 2.5),
                 as.integer(h > 2.0 && p > 3.0),
                 as.integer(!(h < 2.0 || p < 2.0)),
                 as.integer(h > 2.5 && p < 3.5),
                 as.integer(h > 3.5 || (h > 1.5 && p < 1.5)),
                 as.integer(h > 1.5 && (p < 2.5 || h > 3.5)))
        }
        preds  <- vapply(seq_len(n_trials), rule_pred_for_trial, integer(1))
        p_cat1 <- ifelse(preds == 1, 1 - eps_true, eps_true)
        y      <- rbinom(n_trials, 1, p_cat1)

        list(
          variables = list(logit_error_prob = logit_eps_true),
          generated = list(
            ntrials                = n_trials,
            nfeatures              = 2L,
            y                      = as.integer(y),
            obs                    = obs_mat,
            prior_logit_error_mean = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN,
            prior_logit_error_sd   = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD
          )
        )
      }
    )

    psbc_backend <- SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
      mod_rule_stan,
      iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
      chains = 2, adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
    )

    psbc_datasets <- generate_datasets(
      psbc_generator,
      n_sims = n_psbc_iters
    )

    psbc_results <- compute_SBC(
      psbc_datasets, psbc_backend,
      cache_mode     = "results",
      cache_location = here("simdata", "ch14_psbc_cache"),
      keep_fits      = FALSE
    )

    p1 <- plot_ecdf_diff(psbc_results) +
      ggtitle("POSTERIOR SBC for Model B (Phase 6c)",
              subtitle = "True ε drawn from FITTED posterior, not the prior.\nTests local calibration in the high-probability region.")
    p2 <- plot_rank_hist(psbc_results) +
      ggtitle("Posterior SBC rank histogram for Model B")
    print(p1 / p2)
  }
}
```
:::


**Reading this plot.** A flat ECDF here means Model B is calibrated *locally* around the posterior region that the actual data identified. If prior SBC passes but PSBC fails, the sampler is globally unbiased but locally struggles — a sign of geometry issues in the high-posterior region. If both pass, the Stan implementation is sound in both senses.

## Neural Posterior Estimation for Model A

Model A — the particle filter — has an **intractable likelihood**: there is no closed-form expression for $p(y \mid \theta)$. The resampling step introduces a sum over all possible particle trajectories that cannot be evaluated analytically, and the discrete rule space rules out the differentiable marginalisation tricks that Stan exploits. This is not unusual: many of the most cognitively realistic models in the literature share this property. Agent-based models, reinforcement-learning models with discrete latent states, and sequential Monte Carlo approximations all produce data through simulation but resist likelihood evaluation.

**Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE)** is the modern solution to this problem. The core idea is simple: instead of computing $p(y \mid \theta)$ directly, we train a neural network to approximate the posterior $p(\theta \mid y)$ from a large dataset of simulated $(\theta, y)$ pairs. The network never sees a likelihood — it only sees inputs (data $y$) and targets (parameters $\theta$) and learns the mapping from data to posterior by example. Once trained, the network acts as an **amortised** inference engine: given any new observation $y_\text{obs}$, it returns an approximate posterior in milliseconds, without running any additional simulations.

### How NPE Works: the Four Steps

The method proceeds in four conceptual steps. Each step maps directly onto a code block below.

**Step 1 — Define a prior $p(\theta)$.** This plays the same role as in any Bayesian analysis: it encodes your beliefs about the parameters before seeing the data, and it defines the range of parameter values the network will be trained to handle. For Model A we have three parameters: $\varepsilon$ (error probability), $N_\text{particles}$ (working-memory capacity), and $\mu$ (mutation rate). We place weakly informative priors on each, described in detail in the simulator subsection below.

**Step 2 — Simulate a training dataset.** Draw a large number of parameter vectors $\theta^{(i)} \sim p(\theta)$, and for each one run the simulator to produce a synthetic response sequence $y^{(i)} \sim p(y \mid \theta^{(i)})$. The result is a dataset $\{(\theta^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ of input-output pairs. This step is the main computational cost of NPE — it requires many forward simulations — but it is embarrassingly parallel and needs to be done only once.

**Step 3 — Train a conditional density estimator $q_\phi(\theta \mid y)$.** This is the neural network. Its job is to take a response sequence $y$ and output a probability distribution over $\theta$ — not just a point estimate, but a full distribution. The training objective is to maximise the average log-probability of the true parameter under the estimated posterior:
$$\mathcal{L}(\phi) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log q_\phi\!\left(\theta^{(i)} \mid y^{(i)}\right).$$
Maximising this is equivalent to minimising the KL divergence between $q_\phi(\theta \mid y)$ and the true posterior $p(\theta \mid y)$, averaged over the prior predictive $p(y)$. In other words, the network is pushed to assign high probability to the parameter that actually generated each simulated dataset. After seeing enough $(\theta, y)$ pairs, the network learns to read off the correct posterior from any observation.

**Step 4 — Perform inference on the observed data.** Plug $y_\text{obs}$ into the trained network and sample $\theta \sim q_\phi(\theta \mid y_\text{obs})$. These samples are the approximate posterior — no additional simulation, no MCMC warm-up, no gradient computation.

### The Two Neural Components

NPE requires two neural building blocks that work together.

**The embedding network** compresses the high-dimensional observation $y$ into a fixed-size summary vector. Here, $y$ is a sequence of $T$ binary responses, each accompanied by stimulus features (height, position) and feedback. Naively concatenated, this is a $T \times 4$ matrix — too high-dimensional to feed directly into a density estimator without overfitting. A small **multi-layer perceptron (MLP)** processes the per-trial features and learns, through training, which aspects of the response sequence are most informative about $\theta$. Crucially, this is learned end-to-end from the training loss — no hand-crafted summary statistics are needed. The embedding network outputs a compact summary vector $\hat{s}(y)$ that the density estimator then conditions on.

**The normalising flow** is the density estimator itself. A normalising flow is a sequence of invertible, differentiable transformations that maps a simple base distribution (a standard Gaussian) to a flexible target distribution. Because each transformation is invertible, you can both sample from the flow and evaluate its density exactly. The **neural spline flow (NSF)** used here parameterises each transformation with piecewise rational-quadratic splines, making it more expressive than earlier affine coupling layers. The flow's parameters are functions of the conditioning input $\hat{s}(y)$, so the distribution it represents changes for each observation: it is a *conditional* normalising flow, $q_\phi(\theta \mid y)$.

Together: the embedding network compresses $y$ into $\hat{s}(y)$; the flow uses $\hat{s}(y)$ to define a distribution over $\theta$; training adjusts both sets of weights jointly to make that distribution match the true posterior.

::: {.callout-note}
## Comparison with other simulation-based inference methods

NPE belongs to a broader family of **simulation-based** (or likelihood-free) inference methods. The oldest and most transparent member is **rejection ABC**: draw $\theta \sim p(\theta)$, simulate $y_\text{sim} \sim p(y \mid \theta)$, compute a distance $d\!\left(s(y_\text{sim}),\, s(y_\text{obs})\right)$ between hand-crafted summary statistics, and accept $\theta$ if the distance is below a tolerance $\epsilon$. In the limit $\epsilon \to 0$ and with sufficient statistics, this converges to the exact posterior.

Rejection ABC is easy to understand and implement, but it has practical limitations for a model like Model A:

* **Summary statistic choice**: the summaries $s(\cdot)$ must be chosen manually, and their sufficiency is rarely guaranteed. Using block accuracy, per-stimulus accuracy, and lag-1 autocorrelation is reasonable but arbitrary — a different analyst might choose differently, and the posterior depends on the choice. NPE removes this decision by learning the relevant features from data.
* **Acceptance rate**: rejection ABC discards the vast majority of simulations when the prior and posterior are very different (low acceptance rate). Sequential variants (SMC-ABC) improve efficiency but add algorithmic complexity.
* **No amortisation**: rejection ABC pays the full simulation budget for each new subject. NPE pays it once at training time; subsequent inference on any new subject costs milliseconds.
* **Schedule fixing**: because the particle filter is path-dependent, simulations must hold the trial order fixed at the observed schedule and re-randomise only the filter's internal stochasticity. An implementation that re-draws the schedule on each simulation injects spurious trial-order variance and makes posterior recovery harder. This is straightforward to enforce in NPE's fixed-simulator design.

Other neural approaches include **Neural Likelihood Estimation (NLE)**, which learns $p(y \mid \theta)$ rather than the posterior directly and then runs MCMC on the learned likelihood, and **BayesFlow** [@radev2023bayesflow], which uses a different amortised architecture based on coupling flows applied to summary networks. Each has trade-offs; NPE via `sbi` [@tejero-cantero2020sbi; @boelts2024sbi] is our choice because its amortisation is directly useful for the multi-subject analysis later in this chapter.
:::

### Step 1 + 2: The Prior and the Simulator

**Fixing the schedule.** The particle filter is path-dependent: the same parameter values $(\varepsilon, N, \mu)$ produce systematically different response sequences depending on which stimulus appeared on which trial. If the simulator drew a new random trial order for every simulated dataset, two sources of variation would be mixed together in the training data — parameter variation and trial-order variation. The network would then need to disentangle them, which is harder and leads to noisier posteriors. The correct approach is to hold the trial schedule fixed at the observed participant's schedule and re-randomise only the particle filter's internal stochasticity: particle initialisation and Bernoulli response noise. This is what the `npe_schedule` object below does — it is the same schedule used to collect the observed data, and every training simulation uses it.

**The three inferred parameters.** We jointly infer:

* $\varepsilon$ — the error probability, parameterised on the logit scale ($\text{logit}(\varepsilon) \in \mathbb{R}$) so the sampler operates in an unconstrained space. On the logit scale, $\text{logit}(0.1) \approx -2.2$, so a prior of Normal$(0, 1.5)$ on the logit scale places most mass on $\varepsilon \in (0.05, 0.75)$ — covering everything from a near-perfect rule-applier to a near-chance responder.

* $\log(N_\text{particles})$ — the working-memory capacity, parameterised on the log scale so it is positive and well-behaved under sampling. We use a log-uniform prior over $[20, 300]$: each order of magnitude in $N$ is treated as equally likely a priori.

* $\mu$ — the mutation rate, also on the logit scale. The prior Normal$(-3, 1.5)$ on $\text{logit}(\mu)$ has median $\mu \approx 0.047$ and 95% mass on $(0.003, 0.37)$, reflecting the cognitive expectation that most learners replace hypotheses rarely on any given trial.

These three parameters play orthogonal roles. $\varepsilon$ governs how noisily the agent applies its current best rule — high $\varepsilon$ produces near-random responses even when the rule is correct. $\mu$ governs how readily the agent discards that rule — high $\mu$ produces rapid hypothesis turnover, which is beneficial on non-stationary tasks but wasteful on static ones. $N_\text{particles}$ governs the fidelity of the hypothesis distribution — more particles allow finer discrimination between competing rules but are cognitively more costly. On a static task, $\mu$ and $N_\text{particles}$ are expected to be weakly identified; on a task with abrupt category shifts, $\mu$ produces a distinctive sawtooth signature in the learning curve that $\varepsilon$ cannot mimic.

Note that $\mu$ has **no counterpart in Model B**. Model B is a static mixture with no trial-to-trial dynamics; the concept of a mutation rate is structurally absent. The NPE-vs-Stan posterior comparison therefore concerns $\varepsilon$ only — Model B has nothing to say about $\mu$ or $N$.

**The data representation.** Each simulated dataset is a sequence of $T$ binary responses. But the embedding network needs to know more than just the responses — it also needs the stimulus context (height, position) and the feedback on each trial, because the informativeness of any response depends on what stimulus caused it. We therefore concatenate per trial: $[y_t, \text{height}_t, \text{position}_t, \text{feedback}_t]$, giving a $T \times 4$ matrix that is flattened to a vector before being passed to the embedding network. This context is constant across simulations (it is part of the fixed schedule) and is precomputed once.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# The observed schedule — used for ALL simulations (schedule-fix)
npe_schedule <- fit_schedule
npe_obs_mat  <- as.matrix(npe_schedule[, c("height", "position")])
npe_cat_one  <- npe_schedule$category_feedback
n_trials_npe <- nrow(npe_schedule)

# Build the context: [height, position, feedback] per trial
# This is constant across simulations because the schedule is fixed
context_per_trial <- cbind(npe_obs_mat, npe_cat_one)  # (n_trials, 3)

# R-side simulator: theta → response vector
# theta[1] = logit(eps), theta[2] = log(N_particles), theta[3] = logit(mu)
# Uses rule_particle_filter directly (defined earlier in the chapter) so this
# function can be called as soon as it is defined, without waiting for the
# scenario-aware wrapper defined later.
simulate_model_a <- function(theta_vec) {
  eps    <- plogis(theta_vec[1])
  n_part <- max(10L, as.integer(round(exp(theta_vec[2]))))
  mu     <- plogis(theta_vec[3])

  sim <- rule_particle_filter(
    n_particles               = n_part,
    error_prob                = eps,
    mutation_prob             = mu,
    obs                       = npe_obs_mat,
    cat_one                   = npe_cat_one,
    max_dims                  = MAX_RULE_DIMS,
    resample_threshold_factor = RESAMPLE_THRESHOLD,
    quiet                     = TRUE
  )
  as.numeric(sim$sim_response)
}

# Test it
test_theta <- c(qlogis(0.1), log(100), qlogis(0.05))
test_y     <- simulate_model_a(test_theta)
cat("Test simulation: eps =", round(plogis(test_theta[1]), 3),
    ", N_particles =", round(exp(test_theta[2])),
    ", mu =", round(plogis(test_theta[3]), 3),
    ", mean response =", round(mean(test_y), 3), "\n")
```
:::


### Step 2: Generating the Training Dataset

The training dataset is the empirical approximation to the joint distribution $p(\theta) \cdot p(y \mid \theta)$. We draw $N = 20{,}000$ parameter vectors from the prior, simulate one response sequence per parameter vector (using the fixed schedule), and store the $(\theta, y)$ pairs. The network will later be trained to invert this mapping.

A few practical choices matter here:

* **How many simulations?** More is better, but with diminishing returns. For a three-parameter model with a response sequence of length $T \approx 65$, 10–20k simulations are typically sufficient to get reliable posteriors. Increasing to 50k or 100k reduces posterior variance, especially in the tails.

* **Parallel generation.** Each simulation is independent — $\theta^{(i)}$ and $\theta^{(j)}$ do not interact — so we use `furrr::future_map` to parallelise across CPU cores. On a four-core laptop, 20k simulations of a 65-trial particle filter with $N = 100$ particles take roughly 5–10 minutes.

* **Caching.** The training data is saved to disk so subsequent renders do not repeat the simulation budget. Set `regenerate_simulations = TRUE` in the setup chunk to force regeneration (e.g., if the simulator changes).

The training data is generated in R and passed to Python as NumPy arrays. The `context_per_trial` matrix — height, position, and feedback per trial — is constant across simulations and is concatenated with each simulated response vector to form the full observation $x^{(i)}$.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
n_train_sims   <- 20000   # 20k sims; increase to 50k for production
npe_cache_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_training_data.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_cache_file)) {
  cat("Generating NPE training dataset (", n_train_sims, " simulations)...\n")
  set.seed(2026)

  # Draw three parameters from their priors.
  # logit(eps) ~ Normal(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
  # log(N)     ~ Uniform(log(20), log(300))     — log-uniform over [20, 300]
  # logit(mu)  ~ Normal(LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)
  train_logit_eps <- rnorm(n_train_sims, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
  train_log_npart <- runif(n_train_sims, log(20), log(300))
  train_logit_mu  <- rnorm(n_train_sims, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN,  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)
  train_thetas    <- cbind(train_logit_eps, train_log_npart, train_logit_mu)

  # Simulate in parallel — schedule is FIXED (the bug fix)
  train_responses <- future_map(
    seq_len(n_train_sims),
    function(i) simulate_model_a(train_thetas[i, ]),
    .options = furrr_options(seed = TRUE),
    .progress = TRUE
  )

  train_y_mat <- do.call(rbind, train_responses)

  saveRDS(list(thetas = train_thetas, responses = train_y_mat),
          npe_cache_file)
  cat("Training data saved:", n_train_sims, "simulations.\n")
} else {
  cached_train <- readRDS(npe_cache_file)
  train_thetas <- cached_train$thetas
  train_y_mat  <- cached_train$responses
  n_train_sims <- nrow(train_thetas)
  cat("Loaded", n_train_sims, "cached training simulations.\n")
}
```
:::


### Step 3: Training the NPE Inference Network

This is where the neural network learns the mapping from observations to posteriors. The `sbi` package handles the training loop; we need to provide three things: (1) a prior object in sbi's format, (2) the training tensors, and (3) the network architecture.

**The prior in sbi.** The `sbi` library needs an explicit prior object to define the support of $\theta$ — the range of values the network will be expected to handle. We use a `BoxUniform` prior (a box uniform distribution over a hyperrectangle), with bounds set at $\pm 3$ standard deviations for the Normal priors and at the exact log-scale endpoints for the log-uniform $N$ prior. The network will not be reliable outside these bounds, so they should be wide enough to contain all plausible parameter values.

**The network architecture.** `sbi`'s `SNPE` class builds both the embedding network and the normalising flow automatically. We specify `density_estimator = "nsf"` (neural spline flow). Internally, `sbi` constructs:
- An **embedding network**: a small MLP that takes the flattened $[y, \text{context}]$ vector (length $T \times 4$) and outputs a 64-dimensional summary vector.
- A **neural spline flow**: a sequence of coupling layers where each bijection uses rational-quadratic splines parameterised by small neural networks that condition on the embedding.

**Training.** The `inference_obj$train()` call runs mini-batch gradient descent on the loss $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log q_\phi(\theta^{(i)} \mid y^{(i)})$ using the Adam optimiser. Each batch of 256 $(\theta, y)$ pairs contributes a gradient update to both the embedding network and the flow. Training typically converges within a few hundred epochs (each epoch passes through the full 20k dataset once). On a CPU this takes 5–15 minutes; on a GPU it is much faster.

**What does convergence look like?** The `show_train_summary = TRUE` flag prints the training and validation loss at each epoch. A well-trained network shows the validation loss decreasing and levelling off without diverging from the training loss. If the two diverge (overfitting), reduce the network size or increase the training data. If neither loss decreases, the learning rate may be too low.

**Saving the trained model.** We save only the `state_dict` — the learned weights — rather than the full Python object, because Python objects are not safely serialisable across sessions. Loading requires rebuilding the same network architecture and loading the weights back in; the `sbi` documentation covers the exact steps.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (run_intensive_checks) {
  npe_model_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_trained_posterior.pkl")
  
  if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_model_file)) {
    # Convert to PyTorch tensors
    theta_tensor <- torch$tensor(train_thetas, dtype = torch$float32)
  
    # Build data tensor: concatenate [response_t, height_t, position_t, feedback_t]
    # Shape: (n_sims, n_trials * 4)
    x_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_train_sims), function(i) {
      resp <- train_y_mat[i, ]
      as.numeric(t(cbind(resp, context_per_trial)))
    })
    x_mat    <- do.call(rbind, x_list)
    x_tensor <- torch$tensor(x_mat, dtype = torch$float32)
  
    cat("theta shape:", paste(theta_tensor$shape, collapse = " x "), "\n")
    cat("x shape:    ", paste(x_tensor$shape, collapse = " x "), "\n")
  
    # Define prior bounds for sbi.
    # We use a BoxUniform over the joint support of all three parameters.
    # The bounds are set at ±3 SD for the normal priors and the exact
    # range for the log-uniform N_particles prior.
    # theta = [logit(eps), log(N_particles), logit(mu)]
    prior_low  <- torch$tensor(
      c(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN - 3 * LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD,
        log(20),
        LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN    - 3 * LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD),
      dtype = torch$float32
    )
    prior_high <- torch$tensor(
      c(LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN + 3 * LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD,
        log(300),
        LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN    + 3 * LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD),
      dtype = torch$float32
    )
    sbi_prior <- utils$BoxUniform(low = prior_low, high = prior_high)
  
    # Build and train the NPE
    inference_obj <- infer$SNPE(
      prior             = sbi_prior,
      density_estimator = "nsf"   # neural spline flow
    )
    inference_obj$append_simulations(theta_tensor, x_tensor)
  
    cat("Training NPE (this takes a few minutes on CPU)...\n")
    density_estimator <- inference_obj$train(
      training_batch_size = 256L,
      learning_rate       = 5e-4,
      show_train_summary  = TRUE
    )
  
    # Build the posterior object
    npe_posterior <- inference_obj$build_posterior(density_estimator)
    cat("NPE training complete.\n")
  
    # Save the trained model state
    torch$save(density_estimator$state_dict(), npe_model_file)
    cat("Trained NPE saved to", npe_model_file, "\n")
  } else {
    cat("Pre-trained NPE found at", npe_model_file, "\n")
    cat("To use it, rebuild the sbi inference object and load the state dict:\n")
    cat("  inference_obj$build_posterior() -> npe_posterior\n")
    cat("  (see sbi docs for state_dict loading; requires same network architecture)\n")
  }
}
```
:::


### Step 4: Performing Inference on the Observed Data

After training, inference for any new participant is a single forward pass through the network — no MCMC, no additional simulation. This is the **amortisation** payoff: the entire computational cost was paid at training time, and inference now costs milliseconds per subject.

The observed data $y_\text{obs}$ must be formatted identically to the training data: a flattened vector of $[y_t, \text{height}_t, \text{position}_t, \text{feedback}_t]$ for all $T$ trials. The `npe_posterior$sample()` call passes this vector through the trained embedding network, obtains the 64-dimensional summary $\hat{s}(y_\text{obs})$, conditions the normalising flow on it, and draws samples from the resulting distribution over $\theta$.

The returned samples are on the unconstrained (logit / log) scales on which the network was trained. We back-transform with `plogis()` and `exp()` to recover $\varepsilon$, $\mu$, and $N_\text{particles}$ on their natural scales. These 10,000 posterior samples can be treated exactly like MCMC draws: compute means, credible intervals, posterior predictive distributions, or LOO weights from them.

**What to look for.** A well-calibrated NPE posterior for $\varepsilon$ should be centred near the true generating value (0.1 in our simulated example). If the posterior is much wider than the prior, the data are uninformative and the model is not identified for this dataset — expected for short sequences. If it is sharply narrower than the prior, the response sequence contains strong information about the error rate. The posterior for $\mu$ is expected to be wider on static data because a static task cannot distinguish a low-mutation learner from a zero-mutation learner — both will stop replacing hypotheses once the correct rule is found.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Build the observed data tensor (same format as training)
obs_responses <- as.integer(fit_sim$sim_response)
obs_x_vec     <- as.numeric(t(cbind(obs_responses, context_per_trial)))
obs_x_tensor  <- torch$tensor(matrix(obs_x_vec, nrow = 1), dtype = torch$float32)

# Sample from the NPE posterior
n_npe_samples <- 10000L
npe_samples   <- npe_posterior$sample(
  sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_npe_samples),
  x            = obs_x_tensor
)
npe_draws <- as.matrix(npe_samples$numpy())
colnames(npe_draws) <- c("logit_eps", "log_n_particles", "logit_mu")

npe_eps    <- plogis(npe_draws[, "logit_eps"])
npe_n_part <- exp(npe_draws[, "log_n_particles"])
npe_mu     <- plogis(npe_draws[, "logit_mu"])

cat("NPE posterior for ε:           mean =", round(mean(npe_eps), 4),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
cat("NPE posterior for N_particles: mean =", round(mean(npe_n_part), 1),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_n_part, 0.025), 1), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_n_part, 0.975), 1), "]\n")
cat("NPE posterior for μ:           mean =", round(mean(npe_mu), 4),
    "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_mu, 0.025), 4), ",",
    round(quantile(npe_mu, 0.975), 4), "]\n")

p_eps <- ggplot(tibble(eps = npe_eps), aes(x = eps)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for ε (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating ε = 0.1",
       x = "ε", y = "Density")

p_npart <- ggplot(tibble(n_part = npe_n_part), aes(x = n_part)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#0072B2", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 100, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for N_particles (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating N_particles = 100",
       x = "N_particles", y = "Density")

# The μ posterior is expected to be largely prior-like on static data:
# the Kruschke task gives the filter no reason to mutate rapidly, so the
# data are uninformative about mutation rate. On contingent-shift or drift
# data the posterior should sharpen. The prior overlay makes this visible.
prior_mu_draws <- plogis(rnorm(n_npe_samples, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD))
p_mu <- ggplot() +
  geom_density(aes(x = prior_mu_draws), fill = "grey70", alpha = 0.5,
               linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = npe_mu, y = after_stat(density)),
                 fill = "#CC79A7", alpha = 0.7, bins = 40) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "NPE Posterior for μ (Model A)",
       subtitle = "Grey = prior. Red dashed: true generating μ = 0.0 (static task).\nPosterior ≈ prior signals μ is not identified on static data.",
       x = "μ", y = "Density")

print(p_eps / p_npart / p_mu)
```
:::


**Reading the NPE posterior.** Three things to check: (1) the posteriors for $\varepsilon$ and $N_{\text{particles}}$ should contain the true generating values; (2) $N_{\text{particles}}$ should show a meaningful posterior — if it is prior-like, the data are uninformative about working-memory capacity for this task length; (3) the posterior for $\mu$ is **expected to be largely prior-like on static data** — the Kruschke task provides no signal about mutation rate because the correct rule does not change. This is the right result, not a failure. The prior overlay in the $\mu$ panel makes the information gain (or lack thereof) visible. A narrow $\mu$ posterior on non-stationary data would be the substantive finding.

### NPE Validation: SBC + Expected Coverage (Phase 6)

We need to perform calibration checks (see @sec-model-quality-checks). For NPE, the standard diagnostics are marginal SBC (Talts et al. 2018) and expected coverage (Hermans et al. 2022). Because NPE is amortized, running 1000 calibration checks takes seconds — each requires only a forward pass through the trained network.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
n_cal <- 1000L

npe_val_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_npe_validation_results.rds")

if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(npe_val_file)) {
  cat("Running NPE calibration diagnostics (", as.integer(n_cal), " checks)...\n")

  # Generate calibration set: draw theta from prior, simulate x.
  # Three parameters: logit(eps), log(N_particles), logit(mu).
  set.seed(7777)
  cal_logit_eps <- rnorm(n_cal, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN, LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD)
  cal_log_npart <- runif(n_cal, log(20), log(300))
  cal_logit_mu  <- rnorm(n_cal, LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_MEAN,  LOGIT_MU_PRIOR_SD)
  cal_thetas    <- cbind(cal_logit_eps, cal_log_npart, cal_logit_mu)

  cal_responses <- future_map(
    seq_len(n_cal),
    function(i) simulate_model_a(cal_thetas[i, ]),
    .options = furrr_options(seed = TRUE),
    .progress = TRUE
  )
  cal_y_mat <- do.call(rbind, cal_responses)

  # Build calibration data tensors
  cal_x_list <- lapply(seq_len(n_cal), function(i) {
    resp <- cal_y_mat[i, ]
    as.numeric(t(cbind(resp, context_per_trial)))
  })
  cal_x_mat    <- do.call(rbind, cal_x_list)
  cal_x_tensor <- torch$tensor(cal_x_mat, dtype = torch$float32)

  # ── Marginal SBC ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  n_post_samples <- 1000L
  sbc_ranks <- matrix(0L, nrow = n_cal, ncol = 3)
  colnames(sbc_ranks) <- c("logit_eps", "log_n_particles", "logit_mu")

  for (i in seq_len(n_cal)) {
    post_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
      sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_post_samples),
      x            = cal_x_tensor[i - 1L]$unsqueeze(0L)
    )$numpy()

    for (j in 1:3) {
      sbc_ranks[i, j] <- sum(post_samples[, j] < cal_thetas[i, j])
    }
  }

  sbc_df <- as_tibble(sbc_ranks) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "rank")

  # ── Expected Coverage ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  coverage_levels <- seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.05)
  empirical_coverage <- vapply(coverage_levels, function(alpha) {
    covered <- vapply(seq_len(n_cal), function(i) {
      post_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(
        sample_shape = reticulate::tuple(n_post_samples),
        x            = cal_x_tensor[i - 1L]$unsqueeze(0L)
      )$numpy()
      q_lo <- quantile(post_samples[, 1], (1 - alpha) / 2)
      q_hi <- quantile(post_samples[, 1], 1 - (1 - alpha) / 2)
      cal_thetas[i, 1] >= q_lo & cal_thetas[i, 1] <= q_hi
    }, logical(1))
    mean(covered)
  }, numeric(1))

  coverage_df <- tibble(nominal = coverage_levels, empirical = empirical_coverage)
  
  saveRDS(list(sbc_df = sbc_df, coverage_df = coverage_df), npe_val_file)
} else {
  npe_val_res <- readRDS(npe_val_file)
  sbc_df <- npe_val_res$sbc_df
  coverage_df <- npe_val_res$coverage_df
}

p_sbc <- ggplot(sbc_df, aes(x = rank)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 20,
                 fill = "#009E73", alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~parameter, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(title = "Marginal SBC Rank Histograms (NPE, Model A)",
       subtitle = "Uniform = calibrated. U-shape = overconfident. Hump = underconfident.",
       x = "Rank", y = "Count")

p_coverage <- ggplot(coverage_df, aes(x = nominal, y = empirical)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = nominal - 2 * sqrt(nominal * (1 - nominal) / n_cal),
                  ymax = nominal + 2 * sqrt(nominal * (1 - nominal) / n_cal)),
              fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#0072B2", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Expected Coverage (NPE, Model A)",
       subtitle = "Points on diagonal = well-calibrated. Grey band = 2SE.",
       x = "Nominal coverage", y = "Empirical coverage") +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1))

print(p_sbc / p_coverage)

cat("\n--- Coverage summary ---\n")
cat("50% nominal coverage: empirical =",
    round(coverage_df$empirical[coverage_df$nominal == 0.50], 3), "\n")
cat("95% nominal coverage: empirical =",
    round(coverage_df$empirical[coverage_df$nominal == 0.95], 3), "\n")
```
:::


**Reading the validation.** The SBC rank histograms for `logit_eps` and `log_n_particles` should be uniform within sampling noise. The histogram for `logit_mu` requires more care: on static Kruschke data, $\mu$ is expected to be weakly identified, so the posterior is nearly prior-like and the rank histogram should also be approximately uniform — but for a different reason. A uniform SBC histogram for $\mu$ on static data means "the posterior is as uncertain as the prior, which is exactly right given that the data contain no mutation signal." A humped histogram would mean the network is reporting false confidence; a U-shaped histogram would mean it is suppressing genuine variation. The coverage plot should track the diagonal within the grey 2-SE band for all three parameters. If the SBC histogram is U-shaped for any parameter, the posterior is overconfident — increase training data or use a more expressive flow. Both checks benefit enormously from amortization: each of the 1000 calibration checks requires only a forward pass.

### The Main Empirical Result: NPE vs Stan Posteriors

Here is the comparison the chapter has been building toward. We fit the same observed dataset under both Model A (via NPE) and Model B (via HMC) and overlay the two posteriors on $\varepsilon$. **The gap between them is the structural bias you accept when you replace the cognitive theory with a tractable proxy.**


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (exists("npe_eps") && !is.null(fit_rule_stan)) {
  stan_eps <- as_draws_df(fit_rule_stan$draws("error_prob"))$error_prob

  comparison_df <- bind_rows(
    tibble(eps = npe_eps, method = "Model A (NPE, particle filter)"),
    tibble(eps = stan_eps, method = "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)")
  )

  ggplot(comparison_df, aes(x = eps, fill = method, color = method)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Model A (NPE, particle filter)"      = "#009E73",
                                  "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)" = "#E69F00")) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Model A (NPE, particle filter)"      = "#006D4E",
                                   "Model B (HMC, marginalised mixture)" = "#B07D00")) +
    labs(
      title    = "NPE vs HMC: Posterior on ε for Model A vs Model B",
      subtitle = "Red dashed: true generating ε = 0.1\nThe gap between these distributions IS the structural bias of the proxy.",
      x = "ε", y = "Density"
    )

  cat("\n--- Posterior summaries ---\n")
  cat("Model A (NPE) posterior:    mean =", round(mean(npe_eps), 4),
      "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
                       round(quantile(npe_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
  cat("Model B (HMC) posterior:    mean =", round(mean(stan_eps), 4),
      "  95% CI = [", round(quantile(stan_eps, 0.025), 4), ",",
                       round(quantile(stan_eps, 0.975), 4), "]\n")
  cat("True generating ε:           0.1\n")
}
```
:::


### Demonstrating That the Gap Tracks Rule-Set Mismatch

The headline claim — "the gap is structural bias, not inference noise" — requires an experiment. We generate datasets from multiple seeds and compare the NPE-vs-Stan gap. The gap should be small when Model A converges to a rule that is in Model B's candidate set, and large when it does not.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
gap_res_file <- here("simdata", "ch14_gap_demonstration_results.rds")

if (run_intensive_checks && exists("npe_posterior") && !is.null(mod_rule_stan)) {
  if (regenerate_simulations || !file.exists(gap_res_file)) {
    gap_seeds <- c(42, 123, 7, 99, 55)
    gap_results <- map_dfr(gap_seeds, function(s) {
      sched_g <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = s)
      sim_g   <- rule_particle_filter(
        n_particles = N_PARTICLES_FIXED, error_prob = 0.1,
        obs = as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
        cat_one = sched_g$category_feedback, max_dims = MAX_RULE_DIMS, quiet = TRUE
      )

      # NPE inference (amortized — instant)
      obs_g_vec <- as.numeric(t(cbind(as.integer(sim_g$sim_response),
                                       as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
                                       sched_g$category_feedback)))
      obs_g_tensor <- torch$tensor(matrix(obs_g_vec, nrow = 1), dtype = torch$float32)
      npe_g_samples <- npe_posterior$sample(c(5000L), x = obs_g_tensor)$numpy()
      npe_g_eps <- plogis(npe_g_samples[, 1])

      # Stan inference
      stan_dat_g <- list(
        ntrials = nrow(sched_g), nfeatures = 2L,
        y = as.integer(sim_g$sim_response),
        obs = as.matrix(sched_g[, c("height", "position")]),
        prior_logit_error_mean = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_MEAN,
        prior_logit_error_sd   = LOGIT_ERROR_PRIOR_SD
      )
      fit_g <- mod_rule_stan$sample(
        data = stan_dat_g, seed = s, chains = 2, parallel_chains = 2,
        iter_warmup = 800, iter_sampling = 1000, refresh = 0, adapt_delta = 0.9
      )
      stan_g_eps <- as_draws_df(fit_g$draws("error_prob"))$error_prob

      tibble(seed = s,
             npe_mean = mean(npe_g_eps), stan_mean = mean(stan_g_eps),
             gap = abs(mean(npe_g_eps) - mean(stan_g_eps)),
             npe_covers_truth  = (0.1 >= quantile(npe_g_eps, 0.025)) & (0.1 <= quantile(npe_g_eps, 0.975)),
             stan_covers_truth = (0.1 >= quantile(stan_g_eps, 0.025)) & (0.1 <= quantile(stan_g_eps, 0.975)))
    })
    saveRDS(gap_results, gap_res_file)
  } else {
    gap_results <- readRDS(gap_res_file)
  }

  print(gap_results)

  ggplot(gap_results, aes(x = factor(seed), y = gap)) +
    geom_col(fill = "#E69F00", alpha = 0.7) +
    labs(title = "NPE-vs-Stan Gap Across Different Datasets",
         subtitle = "Large gap = converged rule NOT in Model B's set. Small gap = converged rule IS in set.",
         x = "Schedule seed", y = "|NPE mean ε − Stan mean ε|")
}
```
:::


**Reading the gap demonstration.** If the gap varies across seeds — small for some, large for others — the structural bias is driven by rule-set mismatch, not inference noise. This is the expected outcome and the chapter's main empirical contribution.

Because the NPE network is trained once, inference on any new subject's data takes milliseconds. The code pattern:
